introduction: An infection by a very long nematode, Dracunculus medinensis. Contracted through drinking water contaminated with water fleas (cyclops) infected with Dracunculus. Except for remote villages in Rajasthan desert of India and Yemen, the disease is now only seen in Africa, between the Sahara and Equator. Nigeria is one of the few countries with reports of >1,000 new cases a year. Efforts are currently going on to eradicate the disease.

features: As the worm approaches the surface it may be felt as a cordlike thickening
It forms an indurated cutaneous papule
Several hours before the head appears at the skin surface there is local erythema, burning sensation, pruritus, tenderness
Soon after, the papule blisters and a painful ulcer develops, usually on the leg
Ulcer may occur on other parts of the body e.g. the genitalia, buttocks, or arms

investigations: Radiograph of the affected area if osteomyelitis and arthritis (or calcified worms) are suspected

goals: Resolve local inflammation to permit easier removal of the worm
non_drug: Worm extraction by slowly winding it about a match stick or twig, removing 3 - 5 cm daily, with care not to rupture it
Facilitate extraction by placing the affected part in water several times a day
drug: Metronidazole - Adult: 400 mg orally every 8 hours for 7 days; Child: 7.5 mg/kg orally every 8 hours
Mebendazole - Adult: 400 - 800 mg orally daily for 6 days; Child over 1 year: usually 100 mg orally twice daily for 3 days
Ivermectin - Adult: 200 micrograms/kg orally as a single dose; Child: Consult a specialist
Treat or prevent complications with antibiotics
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Metronidazole - Avoid high dose regimens in pregnancy
Metronidazole - Avoid drinking alcohol during treatment and at least 48 hours after
Ivermectin - Oedema (face and limbs), fever, pruritus, lymphadenitis, malaise, hypotension
Ivermectin - Should not be used in the presence of concurrent Loa infection: risk of encephalopathic reaction to dying loa loa microfilariae
Ivermectin - Should not be used in patients with central nervous system diseases (e.g. meningitis): increased penetration of ivermectin into the CNS

prevention: Provide universal access to safe and portable water
In hyperendemic areas, treat the whole population twice yearly with ivermectin
